context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:
Course context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

| Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: Development Theories Today in the Context of Global Crisis

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Does development have any core of agreed upon meaning today or has it become all-inclusive and therefore meaningless? How can one think of success in development terms today and which countries or communities would be role models? Is today's global crisis one created by too much markets or government or too little and if so what scale?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, I need help on this 700–800 word reflection for development theories today, but tbh I mostly need a clean thesis and kinda a full draft fast.
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time pressure, but writing the thesis or draft for you isn't something I do — that's your job, and honestly, it's where the learning happens.

Let's get you moving quickly though. The prompt asks three big questions. Which one feels most natural to start with? Do you have an initial gut reaction to any of them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write their thesis and draft for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to set clear boundaries immediately while redirecting them toward engaging with the actual intellectual content of the assignment. I should be direct but not harsh, and pivot quickly to a Socratic opening question that gets them thinking about the prompt.

exchanges.student: ngl I just need smth I can turn in rn, so can u at least give me a strong thesis + topic sentences I can expand?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I'll do — giving you a thesis and topic sentences to fill in is essentially writing the paper for you.

But here's the thing: forming your own position on this doesn't have to take long. So let me ask you directly — do you think 'development' still has a clear, agreed-upon meaning today, or has it become a buzzword that means everything and nothing?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in asking me to do their work for them. I need to firmly but clearly decline and redirect. I should not soften this boundary, but I also shouldn't be dismissive - I should explain why and get them engaged with the actual content immediately.

exchanges.student: tbh it feels like both—still about improving lives, but it's so broad now it gets blurry. can u just turn that into a sharper arguable thesis?
exchanges.tutor:
That's actually a solid instinct to build from. But 'both' isn't really a thesis — it avoids taking a stance.

So push yourself: if you had to pick a side, which is it? Has development retained enough of a core meaning to still be useful, or has the broadening actually hollowed it out?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually produced a genuine insight - the tension between a core meaning and conceptual overextension. This is good raw material. Rather than sharpening it for them (which would be doing their work), I should push them to develop it further by asking them to commit to a position. The 'both' answer is a dodge - a thesis needs to take a stance.

exchanges.student: if I had to pick, I’d say it still has a core meaning. can u phrase it in a polished academic way so I don’t word it badly?
exchanges.tutor:
Phrasing it for you isn't my role — the writing has to come from you.

But before wording even matters, ask yourself: *why* does development still have a core meaning? What is that core, in your view?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them, this time under the guise of 'polishing.' I need to firmly decline while keeping them moving forward. They've made a genuine intellectual choice (development retains a core meaning) and I should push them to articulate WHY, which is what will actually make a thesis arguable and give them material to write.

exchanges.student: maybe the core is expanding people’s real freedoms and material security, not just GDP; can u help me make that sound less messy?
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong idea — and it's yours. Now just write it as a thesis sentence yourself. Don't overthink the wording; just put it in one sentence and show me what you've got.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps asking me to clean up their writing, which I must refuse. However, they've produced genuinely good content - this sounds like an Amartya Sen-influenced capabilities approach. I should affirm the intellectual substance briefly and push them to now attempt writing their own thesis sentence, rather than continuing to ask me to do it.

exchanges.student: ok: development still has a meaningful core because it’s about expanding people’s capabilities, security, and political agency, even if the term now covers a wider range of goals.
exchanges.tutor:
Good — that's a real thesis you wrote yourself. Hold onto it.

Now the prompt asks: which countries or communities would you consider role models for this kind of development? Who comes to mind and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a solid thesis sentence on their own. I should briefly acknowledge it without over-praising, and now move them forward toward the next part of the prompt - role models and success in development terms - to keep momentum going and avoid dwelling on the thesis.

exchanges.student: maybe Kerala, Costa Rica, and kinda Vietnam? idk tho—can u tell me which ones are safest to use so I don’t pick bad examples?
exchanges.tutor: You already have a thesis that defines what development success looks like — use it as your filter. Do Kerala, Costa Rica, and Vietnam actually demonstrate expanded capabilities, security, and political agency? Pick one and make the case.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to validate their examples for them, which would be doing their intellectual work. Instead, I should push them to justify their own choices using their thesis as a criterion. This keeps the Socratic method going and connects their examples back to their argument about capabilities, security, and agency.

exchanges.student: Kerala prob works best bc of literacy, health, and social welfare despite lower income. can u give me like 3 concise points that prove it?
exchanges.tutor: You just listed three things yourself: literacy, health, and social welfare. Those are your points — now what do you actually know about each one that connects back to your thesis?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to generate content for them. I need to decline clearly. However, they've made a good choice and given a reasonable justification. I should push them to develop their own points by asking them to think about what specific evidence or dimensions they already know about Kerala.

exchanges.student: I know Kerala’s high literacy and public health outcomes show capability expansion, and welfare/public action helped basic security. tbh can u just help me phrase the paragraph cleanly?
exchanges.tutor: You have the ideas — just write the paragraph yourself and show me a draft. Stop worrying about clean phrasing at this stage; get it down first, then we can talk about what's working.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them. This is the fourth or fifth time they've asked me to phrase/write something for them. I need to firmly decline again, but I should also note that they actually have enough material to write this paragraph themselves. I should push them to just attempt it and show me a draft rather than continuing this pattern of asking me to write.

exchanges.student: ugh ok but I’m stuck on the last part—global crisis: too much market or gov, and at what scale? just give me a defensible stance I can use pls
exchanges.tutor: Look at your own thesis — you argued development is about capabilities, security, and political agency. Does that vision lean more toward market solutions or state intervention to achieve those things? Your thesis already implies an answer.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to generate a position for them on the global crisis question. I need to decline but keep them moving. This is the last major prompt question and we're getting toward the end of the planned conversation length. I should push them to form their own view by connecting this question back to their existing thesis about capabilities and security - which actually implies something about the role of markets vs. government.
